title: CABINET PEOPLE
text: CABINET PEOPLE by Norman Prentiss M ira, I nearly got arrested yesterday. I had trouble explaining myself to the police, so be patient. It might not make sense to you either, at first. You know the layout of my building, with “u”- or horseshoe-shaped floors, identical doors lining both sides of each long hallway. Though it’s not exactly a maze, you’re not the only one who got lost the first time you visited. Part of the problem is that our building’s management doesn’t let us put decorations on our doors. And there’s no signage telling which way to turn for each range of numbers. Being cooped up for so long, I decided to take a walk outside yesterday, heading to the stairwell on the opposite part of the floor. From my end of the horseshoe, I walked until I turned one corner, another bowling-alley length until I rounded the next corner, and then I registered the Exit sign beyond the stretch of apartment doors. As I walked, I’d been worried I might run into somebody along the way, since I’d have to flatten myself against the wall to keep distance while they passed; then wondered about the wall itself—if anybody else had touched it, or if I’d touched it myself without thinking, to catch my balance, and the idea made me grab sanitizer gel from my pocket, squeeze out a few drops and spread it over my hands. I was mostly on automatic pilot when I reached the Exit door, turned the handle and shoulder-pushed my way into the stairwell. It was brighter than I remembered. Our hands-off management must have decided to replace a few bulbs, scare away the shadows and gray cement. They might have gone overboard, I thought: as if they’d broken through walls to add windows and opened curtains to admit natural sunlight. And where were the steps? I saw hardwood floors and black-and-white tiles, and no stairs heading up or down. It was my apartment. Without thinking, I’d turned around, must have subconsciously decided against the outside world for the day, piloted back to my own too-familiar dwelling. I washed my hands first thing, as we’re supposed to do. Counted to twenty, stared at the water coming out of the faucet while I tangled my fingers together. I think I laughed at myself, too. How funny that I’d changed my mind and walked back home without realizing it. My schedule’s so confused lately, sleeping at different hours, not driving to work each day—it’s no wonder my brain’s a little addled. The kitchen sink overlooks the open-concept dining area and the living room beyond. I still felt disoriented, and the arrangement of my furniture seemed wrong. I shook my hands dry, wiped them on the front of my trousers, then stepped around to the living area. My recliner was at the wrong angle and too far from the television. I grabbed it low down, lifted and pushed it back where it belonged. The chair’s legs scraped against the hardwood floor, and as I nudged the coffee table aside to make room an empty glass fell off the table and rolled under the flap of fabric along the bottom of the couch. The glass didn’t break, but it was doing that slow back and forth dance, a rhythmic pendulum. I slid the coffee table further out of the way and lay down so I could lift the flap and reach beneath the sofa. God knows what I’d find among the dust bunnies, right? Maybe two other glasses, an extra remote I thought I’d lost, nickels or dimes worth even less than when I first dropped them. The glass rolled along the floor like the rumble of wheels over concrete, the scrape of a door sliding open and shut, the regular tap of footsteps. You remember how a glass might be a musical instrument—wet the rim and run your finger around it, and produce a pure tone almost like a bell or a bird singing? That seemed to be happening now, the glass rim rolling on the floor just out of my reach. The sound had a rasp to it, like a harsh whisper. I pulled my phone from my front pocket and aimed the flashlight app under the couch. Lying flat, I strained and stretched until my fingertips brushed against the glass. Eventually I pinched the rim, stopped the motion. But the whisper continued. I heard panic in it. Somebody who was afraid of getting caught. The person who broke into my apartment while I was out, of course. And if that person whispered out loud, that meant there was an accomplice. The two of them had moved my furniture around, searching for something, and I’d surprised them by coming back early. I froze, not sure how to react. If I stayed there lying on the floor, they might not see me. But my position was vulnerable, too, prone on my stomach and easy for anybody to subdue or attack. At the same time, I struggled to distinguish words whispered in the next room, muffled behind my closed bedroom door. The words would have been my best clues, but the whole time I also stared at the cloth flap that hung down from the frame of the sofa, the curtain I wished I could lift and roll beneath to hide forever. Something about the fabric bothered me, the weave of it, the color of the thread. I heard intruder, the address of the building, urgent pleas to send someone quickly, and I wondered why burglars would call the police on themselves. Yet the sofa fabric is what got my brain working. I’ve spent a lot of time on my couch lately. This wasn’t my couch. Finally, right Sis? I know you figured out the punchline a while ago. But I’m trying to explain the fog in my head—that I could hear these people whisper, the rightful owners of the apartment, and think: “They gave the wrong unit number to the police,” rather than that I was in the wrong place. That I walked into a strange living room and tried to put their furniture right, knocked stuff over, crawled on their floor. And the weave of the sofa fabric began to knock sense into me, nothing else. Not enough sense, though. I stood up, looked around the apartment with the same floor plan as my own but every detail different—a two-tone paint scheme, a larger television bolted to the wall, a hexagon shelf with potted plants in each compartment, and that puzzling sofa substitution—and part of me still wondered how burglars could have worked so quickly, and why they would have replaced my furniture rather than simply stealing it. Phantom redecorators, rather than burglars. Go ahead and laugh, Mira, because I was laughing then, too. My laugh must have sounded crazy. After hearing it, I bet the tenants felt more anxious for the police to arrive. That’s when my virus instincts kicked in. I wasn’t alone. I pulled my mask up to cover my mouth and nose, then rubbed another quick squirt of sanitizer over my hands. “I can hear you whispering,” I said to the closed bedroom door. “You can come out now.” I felt brave. I felt like a conflict mediator offering firm, calming advice. To the tenants, my words probably came across as a command. Like I’d said, “Come out here so I can shoot you.” The door wasn’t fully closed. They must have been peering at me through the gap, periodically asking each other, “What’s that guy doing …?” “I see you there,” I said. “I’m wearing a mask. You can come out.” And they did. Slowly. The guy appeared in the gap first, acted like he needed to squeeze his way through. He wasn’t wearing a mask, which angered me a little. I waved my hand, encouraging him to move a little faster. My hand, not a gun. “Other one, too.” I waved my hand again, indicating the woman, and she followed into the living room, staying close beside him. He leaned at an odd angle, like he was trying to cover her whole body with just his shoulder. They looked familiar to me. They should. As I waited for them to emerge from the back bedroom, I’d half-studied a framed picture on the wall. Clueless or vain? my fogged brain wondered. Because: what kind of burglars would leave their own wedding photograph at the crime scene? I was trying to decide what to say to them, and in retrospect they must have pondered the same. They cast furtive glances at each other, around the apartment, at the floor. At me. An uncomfortable silence lasted a minute or ten—hard to tell which—until we were saved by the bell. More accurately, a knock. Three authoritative raps, the kind they teach in the Academy or in cop films. They announced themselves, too, which is standard procedure. Although my presumptuous intruders moved to open the front door, I cut them off and went there myself. “Hello, officers.” I stepped aside to let them enter. It bothered me that the younger officer was bald, and the older one had a full head of dark wavy hair with just a streak of gray. Seemed out of order—and there’s enough topsy-turvy stuff we’re already having to deal with these days, right? “That’s him,” the woman behind me said. The man pointed at me, his hand high over his head as if I couldn’t see it there. We were in a French farce, without the sexy element. For different reasons, none of us noticed the humor yet. As the couple clung to each other, for the first time I understood the nature of their fear. They weren’t a husband-and-wife cat burglar duo afraid of getting caught. They were more afraid of me than of the police. I considered their behavior, and the sofa fabric. And the kitchen cabinets facing me beside the front door, which had a maple finish rather than the cherry in my unit. My face must have flushed red. Every detail struck me at once, a face slap of logic I’d been denying. I’m guessing I had the kind of face a crazy person makes before they lunge for the knife block on the countertop. Trained officer eyes judged the situation at once, directed their correctional attentions my way… It’s too bad we couldn’t have extended the farce with a tennis ball back-and-forth: “It’s my apartment,” *thunk* “No, it’s our apartment” *thwack* “No it’s mine.” I’d have preferred comedy instead of the humiliation that followed. “I thought…” My shoulders slumped. “I must have…” I turned to the couple who lived here, looked to the ceiling, gestured at the open-concept kitchen and living area. “Confused,” I said, as if that was all the explanation needed. Did I mention how nice the other folks were dressed? I think they used that bedroom as a home office, and they were both in business casual. He wore a dress shirt with rolled-up cuffs, some Dockers-type pants and mesh-knit shoes with leather accents; she wore a striped shirt with an open collar, peach-colored capri pants and matching flats. Maybe they were overdressed for online work sessions, where only head and shoulders showed in the frame, but their wardrobe definitely made a better impression on law enforcement. This wasn’t one of my meeting days, and I’d dressed for a sweaty outdoor stroll—yesterday’s unironed T-shirt with a Spaghetti-O stain over my stomach, and the pair of jeans I’d been wearing all week. I’d rinsed my over-long hair recently, but might not have used shampoo. My broken, confused speech probably hadn’t helped. I needed to raise my eloquence level. “Occam’s razor,” I said. God, Mira, don’t start laughing again. This is the part where you’re supposed to feel sorry for me, okay? I thought if I spoke like an egghead, they’d give me a little more respect, but I’ll admit I came off like a fool. I might even have said “principle of parsimony.” I was trying to explain why I’d been so clueless. I’d started from the wrong premise, ignored anything that contradicted it. Instead of going to the simple and obvious solution—I’d walked into the wrong apartment unit—I piled on preposterous explanations. Multiplying entities without necessity, as the definition goes. Strangers snuck into my apartment and changed furniture, repainted walls and refinished cabinets in a matter of minutes—unlikely even if I’d been drugged or had fallen asleep for a week, a month; even if I’d slipped into a parallel universe where I had different decorating tastes. I got all theoretical, said something about wormholes and altered perception. Over his mask, Officer Grey Streak raised a knowing eyebrow at his partner. That eyebrow said, “drug addict.” “No, really, I do live here.” I sounded like someone repeating a debunked delusion. But by “here,” I meant the building, rather than the specific apartment I now stood in. “I can prove it.” I patted my front pocket. Felt my phone there, but I was looking for my wallet. “Apartment 302,” I said. My wallet was gone. I’d worn my loose pants—the pair I’ve been wearing a lot these days. They’re comfortable around the house. Around my waist, too. The ill-fitting pants were usually fine, since I only met co-workers once a week, my head and shoulders boxed the size of a postage stamp on a crowded meeting screen. Comfortable pants for lounging, but not the best way to secure my belongings. “I seem to have lost…” I trailed off, my credibility shot to hell. They might as well cuff me and drag me to the station. “It’s over there,” the gal tenant said. “On the floor in front of the couch.” I turned around and sure enough, my wallet—right where it had fallen out. You know, when I was crawling around on the floor reaching under a stranger’s sofa. “Thank you.” I practically walked backwards to retrieve it, my hands up where the officers could see them. The woman had stepped away from her husband, or he had stopped bothering to protect her. They were more confident now that the police had arrived. Or maybe because they recognized me. Before the lockdown, we’d have run into each other in the building’s shared courtyard, or at a “community grievance” event where we complained about things the management would never fix—like lights in the hallway, the broken package shelves, the pet-stained rug in the lobby. I was pretty vocal at those gatherings. I’m sure that’s where the couple remembered me from. You’re right, Sis. Sometimes there’s an advantage to being a loudmouth. I found my Driver’s License and held it up. One of the officers lifted a rubber-gloved finger and traced lines in the air above the card. I watched his eyes flit back and forth while reading, then over the top of the card and down while comparing my disheveled, masked face to the five-year-old DMV photo. “302,” I said. When Gray Streak was finished examining it, I offered the card to his partner, who declined. I turned to let the tenants inspect the printed address, but they waved it away. Basically, they seemed ready for me to leave. The lead officer’s tone shifted to wrap-it-up. He summed up the misunderstanding, and none of us contradicted him. “We’re good then?” he said. I responded “yes” immediately, then realized he wasn’t talking to me. The tenants nodded their heads slowly. The woman attempted a reassuring smile. I never actually learned their names, but I’ll make a point to slip an apology note under their door. Later this week, once the trauma’s over. Sure, Mira, their trauma. “We’ll escort you back to your apartment.” A slightly sarcastic emphasis on “your” from Gray Streak, I think. Experienced police officers can get away with that kind of touch. At least he was a little more respectful than when he’d started out: an implied “sir” this time, rather than an implied “scumbag.” The walk back to my apartment felt especially long. The officers social-distanced, which made me feel safer, but also seemed kind of insulting, like they didn’t want to be seen with me. Or I smelled bad. Since I was nervous, I asked about the weather, considering I’d never actually made it outside today. Or the past few days, to be accurate. Neither of them responded. I nattered on about hospitalization and infection statistics that I’d seen scroll beneath The Price is Right, then mentioned we’re almost there after we turned the first corner. “Tough crowd,” I said under my breath, then kinda let the conversation slide for a while. Right, Mira. That’s a new one, me being quiet. Surprised me how well sound carried in those long hallways. The officers moved like they were marching, and their steps landed firm on the thin carpet. They jingled like tambourines—coins or keys in their pockets, I guess. Handcuffs shaking from their belt loops. Loose bullets. “Sorry to have taken up your time, officers.” I started pulling out my keyring before I got to the door, and I imagined diseased particles floating toward the keys, nesting in the metal grooves, spike proteins flailing outward to hook the whorls of my fingertips. Then, of course, I’d absent-mindedly touch my cheek, the corners of my mouth or eyes. “Here we are. 302. Home sweet, and all that.” After a few misses, I jammed the key in the lock and turned it. Wouldn’t it be funny if this was the wrong place, too? Maybe that’s why the officers waited instead of moving along to the next dire emergency. I used the key to push down the handle, then shouldered the door open. The apartment smelled like gravy and armpits. With the blinds closed and the sky overcast outside, the place was mostly dark. When I flipped the switch with my elbow, things didn’t get much brighter. Of the three track lights in my kitchen, only one of them worked. I needed to get new bulbs, but also suspected an electrical fault in one of the sockets. Who knows if Management would send an electrician—and with the virus still hot, I didn’t want anybody coming into the apartment. Not that I could stop Officer Gray Streak from entering. Even with the mask over his nose, I could hear his disapproving sniff. I started washing my hands, waving them between the faucet and the mounting pile of dishes in the sink. “Happy Birthday” went through my head two times before I finished. It didn’t feel like my birthday at all. “Hollings, step in here a second.” That tone signaled the kind of moment we’re trained to dread. I’d been getting away with it, whatever my crime was supposed to be—accidental trespassing, I guess—and the officers wrapped things up, prepared to leave the scene, and suddenly one of them paused, high alert, called his partner for backup. Next thing you know, they’re unlatching holsters, raising weapons, screaming and bulldozing forward. A takedown strikes in an instant: you’re on the ground, flipped over, arms pulled behind your back, then the click of handcuffs grinding over your wrists. No, Sis, that didn’t actually happen. I’m trying to tell you what it felt like in the moment. What I was afraid of, okay? “Is there a problem, officers?” My innocent-voice cracked. Although I’d turned off the water, a deep gurgle rose from dregs filtering past the sink’s stopper. Like the growl of a rabid dog. A sewer demon. The younger officer walked in. His cap was in one hand, a sheen of sweat beading on his scalp, making me wonder if he had a temperature. The other hand hovered near his gun holster, but he hadn’t shifted into attack mode. Guess I didn’t intimidate him. “Do you see it?” the older partner asked. “I don’t see it.” The bald guy shrugged. I couldn’t figure what Officer Grey Streak was asking, either. Were they supposed to be finding drugs or stolen merchandise? Did secret signs in my apartment identify me as a lunatic or a ninja assassin? “How could he…?” The senior officer trailed off, coaxing his partner to complete the clue. “God,” the younger officer finally said, breaking his silent observer mode. “How could this guy have mistaken that nice, clean apartment for this dump?” Yeah, Mira, I’m grateful he didn’t say “shithole.” But, you know, I was standing right there. You’d think they’d use a little more tact. I felt like I had to explain myself. “Probably this damn thing.” I pointed to the pale blue mask over my nose and mouth, circled my hand over my whole face as if the mask covered my eyes, too. “Throws everything off.” The officers squinted, offered each other knowing nods. They’d identified the lunatic they were looking for. Unmasked me, so to speak. At least that meant they were finished. Gray Streak performed a quick stay-out-of-trouble speech, half of it delivered over one shoulder as he turned to leave. This all must seem kind of funny to you, Mira, especially since I let myself play the fool in the telling of it. But I think I’m onto something here. A new syndrome, maybe. The kind of muddled thinking that comes from the disruption of lockdown, going long stretches without human contact. I honestly couldn’t think the way I usually do. I got stuck. Yes, I think it’s something new. Did you ever know anybody who got stuck like that? Their brain following the wrong track, and they couldn’t get off it? Oh, right. I guess you did. Gran. Gran and the Cabinet People. I’m sorry to bring it up. I thought I was just telling a funny story about my police encounter. Your idiot brother, right? Well, I don’t want to keep you. I’ll check back sometime next week, okay? In the meantime, Sis, as my officer buddy would say: stay out of trouble. # Hope you haven’t been worried about me, Mira. It’s been nearly a month since we talked. I gathered you didn’t want to get into it about Gran last time. That’s why I ended the call so abruptly. We should talk about her now, since I’m not sure we ever did. Not seriously. Is that okay? I’ll take your silence as a reluctant Yes. Listen, I know I didn’t react well when you told me about the Cabinet People. It’s just that I’ve known Gran since we were kids, and she was always sharp as a whip. We could never pull one over on her, right? Like that trick with the cereal bowl, or when I pretended to— Okay, you get the picture. So when you told me she was having hallucinations, I thought it was a temporary thing. Something she could be talked out of. We all listen to reason eventually? That’s what I was thinking at the time. She was fine whenever I spoke with her on the phone. Same old Gran. Older, sure, moving a bit slower so not catching me on the first few rings. But her mind was fine. So I’d been kind of dismissive of your concerns back then, which was unfair. You were the one who visited her the most—and later, you were generous enough to open your home to her and care for her. I mean, I couldn’t take her in , what with my job and my one-bedroom apartment. I felt guilty about that, and was always grateful that you stepped up. I guess I could have shown that gratitude more. Like, maybe, listening to you. Deferring to your judgment. Don’t get mad, but...I’d insisted so long that you’d been worrying too much, had my stubborn mind made up about it. To the point where, when I finally knew better, I couldn’t bring myself to admit it. I asked you not to get mad, Mira. I’ll say it now, okay? You were right. But it was just that one time. That’s how I explained it to myself after I saw Gran in her apartment. She was always asking me to visit, and I could never seem to find the time. During one of our phone calls, I let slip about a long weekend, my not having any special plans, and that’s all it took. She wouldn’t hang up until I promised to see her for Saturday dinner. The visit was great. Her second-floor apartment was just like I remembered it when we did sleepovers as kids—though Pops wasn’t sitting by the radiator, his chair aimed at the TV and a full ashtray balanced on one of the arms. Without the clouds of smoke he provided, the place was clean and fresh. Gran swizzled up a ham steak with a side of yams and brown-and-serve rolls, and everything was perfect. What’s Mira been going on about? Maybe my sister’s the crazy one. I can’t lie. That’s what went through my head at the time. Gran drizzled some Hersey’s syrup over store-brand vanilla ice cream, and we enjoyed dessert in the den. She mentioned how I always had a sweet tooth. “I often think of you sitting right there,” pointing to the same spot I occupied on the couch, “stirring cookie batter, so proud at how careful you were being, since you kids were supposedly too messy to bake anything at home.” It was a sweet memory of a time that was precious to me, and more so because Gran remembered it so clearly. She gave my knee an affectionate tap, leaned closer, and I waited for her to share another wonderful moment from my childhood. “There are people living in my cabinets,” she whispered, like someone telling a ghost story, or admitting a private shame. Her eyes moved from my face to the kitchen and back. To the kitchen where she’d just baked dinner. A wary, terrified gaze at the same cabinet where she’d retrieved the can of chocolate syrup. My heart sank. She’d made me a kid again, with that nostalgic dinner. Affectionate memories had been rushing back in an overwhelming torrent. In the afterglow of dinner and ice cream, I couldn’t separate from my younger self—the kid who loved his grandmother, and candy, but also had that sense of wonder, who believed in ghosts or other monsters, who could look at an ordinary kitchen cabinet and imagine evil beings contorted behind thin wooden doors. The cabinets didn’t lock. Long fingernails would scratch at the wood, push the doors slightly open to let dark, deep-set eyes peer through the gap. A drop of saliva drips from blood-hungry mouths; teeth gnash in anticipation. I was too afraid to look toward the cabinets. But a lot could happen when I wasn’t looking. I thought I heard the shift of glass jars, ceramic saucers and bowls. The creak of hinges. I stared at Gran’s face instead, her eyes wide behind thick glasses. She heard what I was hearing. She’s been living with this fear every minute of the day. And night, too. Because who knows what Cabinet People could do at night, without watchful eyes to keep them in their place? Gran’s hand was on my knee again, and she squeezed tight. “When they use the toilets,” she said, “they don’t flush afterwards.” Well, Mira, that broke the spell. She’d made that same absurd comment to you, and I couldn’t stop laughing that time you quoted it to me. “Gran can’t have been serious,” I remember telling you. You’re right, Sis. It wasn’t funny. When Gran said the same to me, I felt like she was another person. Her face was perfectly blank. Her eyes seemed empty, her mouth tight like she would never smile again. That’s what you saw, too, I realize now. I should have supported you sooner when you talked about getting her help. Should have encouraged your plan to move Gran out of that apartment, at least. But I was in denial. Tell me: did those uninvited guests pack themselves into her suitcases and travel with her to your house? Did she see the Cabinet People again, once she’d moved to your place? Or did you notice if her eyes darted suspiciously to the cabinets in your remodeled kitchen? —the ones over the counter, or the larger ones beneath the sink where two of those people might fit, their limbs tangled around bends in the p-trap plumbing tubes. She never reached for a steak knife and held it tight to her chest with both frail hands, listening for a scratch behind wood, waiting for a door to spring open? No. Of course not. Gran slept better in those final months with you. We both have to believe that. I can’t say what got me on this train of thought. Mostly my weird experience last month when I walked into the wrong apartment, and the logical part of my mind seemed to switch off. Same with Gran, right? There’s no way those Cabinet People could have gotten into her apartment. Even if they did, they wouldn’t logically be able to fit with the other items in the full cupboards, not to mention the dividing shelves. And why wouldn’t they prefer more spacious living quarters? Seriously, how would they sleep comfortably? When they were awake, how would they amuse themselves? My reason for calling today might have something to do with a dream I had. Before bedtime last night, I’d been watching a magic show on television. The same program was playing in my dream. Yeah, all the money I spend on cable and streaming, and even my dreams have repeats. Eventually we got the ol’ saw-the-lady-in-half trick. Slightly more fun than a card trick, but pretty easy to figure out the secret. The assistant’s super thin, and super nimble, and the box is bigger than the audience realizes. The saw’s not too sharp or solid, either. In my dream, the assistant is a normal sized woman, and the box seems especially narrow. She climbs inside with the magician’s help, and it’s a tight fit. He closes the lid. Her head sticks out one end, long dark hair hanging down, nearly touching the stage. Bare feet stick out the other end, toes wriggling as if being tickled. They look real. The magician has pale skin and the kind of salt-and-pepper goatee favored by morticians in low-budget movies. He wears the typical magician’s tux with a cape flowing behind—hidden pockets everywhere, I’m sure, to conceal rabbits and doves and endless flowing scarves. The magician grabs at the air, and a handsaw appears. He bends the blade, making it sing. There’s a notch on the side of the box, indicating the midpoint where the saw should go. As he steps over to the boxed assistant, her head turns and she starts to struggle. “There’s someone in here with me.” The magician positions himself behind the box, then lowers the saw into the notch. “Don’t give away the secret.” He flicks the cape behind him with a flourish. Begins sawing. The woman starts screaming, her hair lashing the stage floor as she twists her neck. “Someone’s crawling over me,” she says. It occurs to me that the box is like a coffin. A coffin is a cabinet that fits into the ground. The hinged lid, once closed, should never be opened. If there’s another person in the magic box, the two of them must be twisted together like bread ties. Like braided bodies. I’m dreaming, but I can’t escape my lockdown paranoia. They are too close together, sweating their disease into each other pores. The saw moves back and forth. “Which one of us,” the assistant cries out. “Which one of us are you cutting?” That’s when I woke up. My bedroom was dark and stuffy. I couldn’t see or breathe or move—as if a lid pressed down on me, holding me in place. I heard the scratch of a saw’s teeth on wood. Fingernails digging into the grain. When did I get out of that box, Mira? It’s hard to say, since I might have been asleep for part of the time. Even after I was fully awake, my mind remained fuzzy. I turned on the light, walked from one room to the next, and couldn’t shake the feeling that something had changed. Like it wasn’t my apartment anymore. Or, at least, not only my apartment. I haven’t opened them, Mira. Not any of them. I’m afraid to. Tell me again, please. Gran stopped seeing the Cabinet People once she moved in with you. Didn’t she? In my experience, not many writers can successfully mix dashes of Lovecraft's pulpy cosmic horror with pulpy crime/noir sensibilities. The late great Tom Piccirilli comes to mind as one of the best at it. As you've already seen in this collection, Richard Thomas is more than capable of such a lethal stew, and so is Anthony J. Rapino, as he closes out the first installment of Liminal Spaces with his own spin on organized crime and watery abominations in “The Black Door.”
